title: practical english usage - Part 114
content:
I haven't seen the sun for days.  Do you trust the government?

there is only one in our part of the world (e.g. the government).

only one (e.g. the sun, the moon, the earth, the world, the universe, the future) or The  listener may know which  one we  mean because there is no choice - there is the 2    = 'the   only    one(s)   around' the;   my,    your,          this,    that,           Section12 Determiners:       a/an   and           etc;       etc grammar •  137   more about a/an Andy lives in an old house.

There's a police car outside. My brother's married to a doctor.

We   can use a/an when we  mention one person or thing for the first time.

a  one person or thing ‘ one’ . It has several common uses.

A/an does not add much to the meaning of a noun - it is like a weak form of 3 uses    of  a/an For expressions like a good two hours, ►     130.7.

but not              or a salt     an offices a secretary an office We   normally use ɑ/ɑn  only with singular countable nouns.

2  a/an    with    singular      countable        nouns For more detailed information, ►     119-120.

but notanenergy  several energy (                           ,              energies) but nota weather   fourweathers weather (                           ,                         ) but not                                                                    but not wool (          a wool, two wools) water (          a water, three waters) we  do not usually see as separate objects.

Uncountable nouns are the names of materials, liquids and other things which a plan - two plans a cat - three cats a secretary - four secretaries can count.

Countable nouns are the names of separate objects, people, ideas, etc which  we 1  countable        and   uncountable         nouns 137 more    about     a/an For on the bus, at the dentist's, etc, ►     141.5.   For other advanced points, ►     141 .

For the town, the country, the sea, the mountains, etc, ►     141.4.

I've found the /ði:/  present for Angela!

He's the /di:/ - just a moment - deputy assistant vice-president.

to stress the noun phrase, even if the noun begins with a consonant.

We   sometimes pronounce a stressed /ðiɪ/    before a hesitation, or when we  want the one-pound coin /ðə     ,wʌn  ,paond 'kɔɪn/ the university /ðə     ,ju:ni'vɜ:səti/ a vowel.

And we  pronounce /ðə/     before a consonant sound, even if it is written as the hour /di: 'auə(r)/   the MP  /ði:  'em 'pi:/ We   pronounce /ðiɪ/    before a vowel sound, even if it is written as a consonant.

The  choice between /ði:/  and /ðə/     depends on pronunciation, not spelling.

the ice /ði: aɪs/   the snow /ðə     snəu/ Compare: grammar •  137   more about a/an a university /ə ju:nɪ'vɜɪsəti/   a one-pound coin /d ,wʌn  ,paond 'kɔin/   -» And we  use a before a consonant sound, even if it is written as a vowel.

an hour /ən 'auə(r)/   an MP  /ən em 'pi:/ an before a vowel sound, even if it is written as a consonant.

The  choice between a and an depends on pronunciation, not spelling. We   use a rabbit a lemon an elephant an orange the article a (/ə/) changes to an /ənʌ   Compare: We   do not normally pronounce the sound /ə/   before a vowel. So before a vowel, 7 a  and   an:  the   difference For details, ►     136.

She lives in the big house over there. (The hearer knows which  one.) She lives in a big house. (The hearer doesn’ t know which  one.) one I mean’ . Compare: Instead of a/an, we  use the when we  want to say 'You and I both know which 6 a/an   and   the not He's a friend of mine, (     He's a my friend.) structure a... of mine/yours, etc (►   177).

A/an cannot be used together with a possessive. Instead, we  can use the not It's a good car. It's good, (     H's-agoed.) Compare: A/an cannot normally be used with an adjective alone (without a noun).

5 when     a/an   is  not   used:   adjectives       alone;    possessives not I used my shoe as a hammer, (     .. .-os-hammer/) not She's an engineer, (     She's-engineer!) things are used (see above).

And we  do not leave out a/an when we  say what jobs people have, or how not three-quarters of a pound (     three-quarters qf-peund) not You shouldn't go out without a coat, (     .. .-witheut-eeat/) not 'Lend me your pen.' 'I haven't got a pen. (     L-havenHrgptrpen!)' or after fractions.
